coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of (anatase/bronze-type)-TiO2/carbon dot polymorphic structures and their photocatalytic activity for H2 generation
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Sovann
Kubota, Yuta
Lei, Wenwei
Suzuki, Norihiro
Nakata, Kazuya
Terashima, Chiaki
Matsushita, Nobuhiro
Fujishima, Akira
Katsumata, Ken-ichi
coredata.dc:description: We report the one-pot synthesis of anatase/bronze-type ((A/B)-TiO2) and carbon dots (CDs) via a simple solvothermal method using only TiCl4 and ethylene glycol. Control of the reaction temperature and duration enabled the compositions of pure (B) phase, (A/B)-TiO2/CDs, and (A)-TiO2/CDs to be tuned. A series of experiments on the synthesis conditions was carefully conducted at various temperatures (150–200 °C) for various reaction times (5–36 h). Pure phase (B)-TiO2 was obtained at 150 °C for all reaction times (5 to 36 h). The (B)-phase was transformed to anatase at temperatures from 170 °C for reaction times longer than 12 h. At the same time, CDs were formed from the decomposition of ethylene glycol at temperatures greater than 160 °C and long reaction times (>12 h). The photocatalytic activities of the composite catalysts were evaluated by H2 generation from a water-ethylene glycol mixed solution under UV–visible light. H2 generation rates were observed in this order: (A/B)-TiO2/CDs > (A)-TiO2/CDs > (B)-TiO2 > (A)-TiO2. This high photocatalytic activity for (A/B)-TiO2/CDs is attributed to effective charge-separation and broad-light absorption. The developed method will be important for the facile synthesis of (A/B)-TiO2/CD materials and has more potential than conventional multi-step methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bronze-type-TiO2
Carbon dots
Heterostructure
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by a Grant-in-Aid for JSPS Fellows ( 18F18337 ) from the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS). The authors thank Technical Department, Ookayama Materials Analysis Division, Tokyo Institute of Technology, for performing the FE-TEM observation. S. Khan would like to thank JSPS for supporting his postdoctoral research (standard-P18337).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematics of synthesis procedure, (b) Long-range XRD spectra for samples synthesized at different temperatures (150 °C to 200 °C for 24 h); (c) short range XRD of (b) from 22 to 30°.
Raman shift of synthesized samples at different reaction temperatures from 150 °C to 190 °C for 24 h-reaction time, and the prepared (A)-phase.

Photographs of crude solutions after 24 h of reaction time under (a) room light and (b) 360 nm UV light. (c) PL spectra of Ti170/water excited at 300 nm, 350 nm, 400 nm and 450 nm. (d) PL emission of (c) with normalized intensities, and (e, f) PL spectra of (A)/water, Ti170 [(A/B)-TiO2/CDs] and 170 °C-thermal treated EG/water (CD solution) with excitation at UV (350 nm) and NIR (800 nm).
SEM images of catalysts synthesized for a 24 h reaction time at reaction temperatures of 150, 160, 170, 180 and 190 °C.

Low-resolution TEM images of synthesized (a) Ti170, (b) Ti180 and (c) Ti190. (d, g) HR-TEM images of two different places, (e, f) selected area diffraction index of (d, h) and (i) selected area diffraction index of (g).
XPS spectra of Ti170 sample: (a) full-range spectra, and (b)–(d) high-resolution spectra of Ti 2p, O 1 s and C 1 s.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) optical bandgaps from Tauc plots versus photon energy absorbed.

(a) H2 generation over 8 h with different catalysts. (b) H2 production rates from catalysts produced with different reaction temperatures. (c) Photocurrent density response to light. (d) Proposed mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity over the (A/B)-TiO2/CDs heterostructure.
(a) H2 generation and (b) H2 production rates over Ti170 loaded with non-noble metals of Ni, Cu and Co. (c) Recycle experiment for H2 generation over the Ti170/Cu catalyst.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of (anatase/bronze-type)-TiO2/carbon dot polymorphic structures and their photocatalytic activity for H2 generation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Technology
Faculty of Industrial Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the one-pot synthesis of anatase/bronze-type ((A/B)-TiO2) and carbon dots (CDs) via a simple solvothermal method using only TiCl4 and ethylene glycol. Control of the reaction temperature and duration enabled the compositions of pure (B) phase, (A/B)-TiO2/CDs, and (A)-TiO2/CDs to be tuned. A series of experiments on the synthesis conditions was carefully conducted at various temperatures (150–200 °C) for various reaction times (5–36 h). Pure phase (B)-TiO2 was obtained at 150 °C for all reaction times (5 to 36 h). The (B)-phase was transformed to anatase at temperatures from 170 °C for reaction times longer than 12 h. At the same time, CDs were formed from the decomposition of ethylene glycol at temperatures greater than 160 °C and long reaction times (>12 h). The photocatalytic activities of the composite catalysts were evaluated by H2 generation from a water-ethylene glycol mixed solution under UV–visible light. H2 generation rates were observed in this order: (A/B)-TiO2/CDs > (A)-TiO2/CDs > (B)-TiO2 > (A)-TiO2. This high photocatalytic activity for (A/B)-TiO2/CDs is attributed to effective charge-separation and broad-light absorption. The developed method will be important for the facile synthesis of (A/B)-TiO2/CD materials and has more potential than conventional multi-step methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the natural crystallized phases of titanium dioxide (TiO2), bronze-type TiO2 (B-TiO2) is the least-studied phase, compared to the well-known anatase (A) and rutile (R) phases, because (B)-TiO2 is a metastable phase that requires specific conditions for synthesis of its pure form. Much research to date has struggled to develop new routes for the synthesis of the pure (B)-TiO2 phase with higher stability [1–4]. The synthesis routes have been well developed recently, so that the potential of (B)-TiO2 for energy storage and conversion applications (e.g., batteries and photocatalysis) has become evident [2,3,5–8]. However, it is similar to anatase, rutile or other oxide-based semiconductors that low efficiency of photocatalytic activities due to fast recombination rate of photo-excited electron-hole pairs was always a main problem. Many attempts on development of highly active photocatalysts based on nanostructure designs and defect/bandgap engineering have been widely reported [5,6,9].

Many successful works have reported on new approaches to improve the photocatalytic activity of (B)-TiO2 based on nanostructure engineering. For example, with large surface areas of porous microspheres composed of ultra-thin nanosheets, (B)-TiO2 has exhibited exceptional photocatalytic activity for CO2 reduction to methane and methanol compared to that for (A)-TiO2 and commercial P25 [5]. Beside nanostructure engineering, controlling defects (e.g. oxygen vacancies and impurity doping) was also among potential methods to improve photocatalytic activities of TiO2 [9,10]. Kong et al. studied on effects of bulk and surface defects on photocatalytic activities of TiO2, and it was found that reducing ratio of bulk to surface defects improved charge-separation on TiO2 particles, resulting enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [9]. In addition to defect engineering, constructing heterostructures between two or more materials or phases was conventionally used to improve photo-induced electron-hole separation. In particular, heterostructures of (A/B)-TiO2 have also proven to have good band alignment to separate photogenerated electrons and holes, which results in significantly improved photocatalytic efficiency [6,11]. Therefore, recent focus on facile methods to synthesize this heterostructure has attracted much interest.

Yang et al. synthesized (B)-TiO2 nanofibers with (A) nanocrystal shells via a three-step hydrothermal method (H2Ti3O7 fiber → H2Ti3O7@(A) → TiO2(B)@(A)) [6]. This structure showed superior UV-photocatalytic activity toward sulforhodamine B over pure (B)-TiO2 and (A). Multi-compositions of rutile/(A)/(B)-TiO2 have recently been developed by the proton exchange of hydrothermal-treated sodium titanate nanotubes with a post-annealing step [12]. This structure exhibited high photocatalytic activity for H2 production that was 19 times higher than that with pristine TiO2 nanotubes [12]. Wang et al. synthesized (A/B)-TiO2 heterostructure via a simple thermal treatment from 450 to 550 °C. Thermally treated (A/B)-TiO2 exhibited higher photocatalytic H2 production than the individual phases and that of P25 [8]. However, multiple-step synthesis and thermal treatment may not be preferable for large-scale production due to complexity of the synthesis and the high energy consumption. Alternatively, a one-pot method is always considered as a facile synthesis with significant potential for industrial purposes. In this study, we report a single-step solvothermal method to synthesize (A/B)-TiO2 from TiCl4 in ethylene glycol (EG) solution. Using controlled reaction parameters, EG was decomposed to form carbon dots (CDs) during thermal treatment, which resulted in an (A/B)-TiO2/CD heterostructured photocatalyst by a single-step process.

Carbon/semiconductor composites were among excellent heterostructured materials, which demonstrated higher photocatalytic efficiency due to improved visible light utilization and charge-separation [13,14]. Among carbon materials, CDs have significant potential as light-harvesting materials because of their broad light-absorption capability (from UV–visible to near-infrared (NIR) wavelength), stability and non-toxicity [15]. Hybrid structures of TiO2/CDs have been widely reported as active photocatalyst complexes under UV–visible-NIR light [16,17]. CDs 1) act as photogenerated electron reservoirs, which improve the charge separation on a TiO2 photocatalyst, 2) act as visible-light photosensitizers, whereby photogenerated electrons from π conjugation (similar to a dye-sensitizer) of CDs can transfer to TiO2 for further photocatalytic reactions, and 3) have upconverting fluorescent properties; CDs enable absorption of the NIR spectrum and emit visible light, which is further utilized by a TiO2 photocatalyst [16,17]. A hybrid structure of TiO2 and CDs could be an excellent choice for solar energy-harvesting for photocatalytic reactions. However, a two-step process is generally used to obtain this heterostructure, where TiO2 or CDs are separately prepared, and then finally mixed together. This process is time-consuming, and typically the separation of QDs (light and small) for further use is not easily achieved.

Here, we propose a new approach to produce a (A/B)-TiO2/CD heterostructure using a single-step solvothermal method. This method consists of the hydrolysis of TiCl4 salts in EG solution under high temperature treatment (160 to 180 °C) for longer reaction times (>12 h). Simple reaction-parameters (e.g. reaction temperature and duration) are controlled in an attempt to produce several phases of the materials such as pure (B)-TiO2, (A/B)-TiO2/CDs and TiO2/CDs. The synthesized materials were then investigated with respect to photocatalytic H2-production from EG-water solution under UV–visible light radiation.

(A/B)-TiO2/CDs were synthesized by solvothermal methods with a slight modification of a previous procedure [2] (see represented schematics in Fig. 1 (a)). 0.5 mL of TiCl4 (Sigma Aldrich Inc., USA) was dissolved in 30 mL of EG (C2H6O2, Sigma Aldrich Inc. USA) by sonication for 20 min and stirring for 60 min. 0.4 mL of NH3 solution (28%, Sigma Aldrich Inc. USA) was then added to the TiCl4-EG solution and stirred for 30 min. Ammonia was selected as an additive to promote the growth and stabilize the sheet-like structure of (B)-TiO2 formed [2]. This transparent mixture was transferred into a 40 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated up to 150–200 °C for 5 to 36 h. Samples synthesized at 150, 160, 170, 180, 190, 200 °C for 24 h are termed as Ti150, Ti160, Ti170, Ti180, Ti190 and Ti200, respectively. After reaction, catalytic powder was collected by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 30 min and washing 3 times with ethanol and water. The obtained powder was then dried in an oven at 68 °C for 24 h before further characterization. As a control, anatase was also synthesized by a solvothermal method [18], followed by annealing at 450 °C for 2 h under ambient conditions.

The morphology and nanostructures of the catalysts were investigated using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; JSM-7600F, JEOL, Japan) and field emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM; JEM-2010F, JEOL, Japan). Crystal structures were analyzed by using X-ray diffraction (XRD; Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) and Raman scattering spectroscopy (NRS-5100, Jasco, Japan). Optical properties were studied using photoluminescence (PL)/photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectroscopy (LS5, Perkin Elmer, USA), and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (V-670, Jasco, Japan). Elemental compositions and surface area were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; ESCA-3400, Shimadzu, Japan). The photocatalytic activity of the catalysts was evaluated by H2 generation from EG-water solution under broad spectrum light from a Hg-Xe lamp (UV–visible light). 50 mg of catalyst was dispersed into 50 mL of EG (20 vol%) aqueous solution by sonication for 20 min. In some cases, various amounts of metal nitrates such as Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (Sigma Aldrich Inc., USA), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (Wako Chemical Corp., Japan) and Co(NO3)·6H2O (Wako Chemicals Corp., Japan) were added to form metal-loaded catalysts. The mixture was then poured into a 300 mL glass cell covered with a quartz window and bubbled with N2 gas for 20 min to remove oxygen contamination. Light was then irradiated onto the top of the cell. 1 mL of gas was sampled every hour and injected into a gas chromatograph equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD-GC; GC-2014, Shimadzu, Japan) to quantify the gas composition. Photocurrent density of catalyst films were measured by VersaSTAT 4 system (METEK, USA) at constant voltage of 0.8 V. Catalyst films were prepared by dispersion of 20 mg of catalyst into 5 mL of ethanol by 10-minute sonication and 30 stirring. Then, 3 mL of mixture was dropped on the FTO glass and swiped by doctor- blade to spread the catalyst on the FTO’s surface. Catalyst films were dried at 68 °C for 24 h before characterizations.

XRD spectra presented in Fig. 1(b) show the crystal structures of samples synthesized at various temperatures (150 to 200 °C) for 24 h of solvothermal reaction. Pure (B)-phase was obtained at lower temperature (150–160 °C). A unique broad peak at 14.79° was identified as the monoclinic phase of (B)-TiO2, which was composed of the (001) plane at 14.19° and (200) plane at 15.20°, according to JCPDS#46-1238 for (B)-TiO2. A mixture of (A)- and (B)-phases was observed at 170–180 °C. However, at higher temperatures (190–200 °C), pure (A)-phase was completely formed. XRD peaks of the Ti190 sample matched well to JCPDS#01-0562 for tetragonal (A)-phase. Magnified XRD spectra at short ranges (20 to 40°) showed broad peak at 24–26° shifted slowly to higher angles when the synthesis temperature was raised from 150 °C to 200 °C. The most intense XRD peak of (A)- and (B)-TiO2 appeared at 25.28° (101) and 24.98° (110), respectively. Therefore, the shift was due to the presence of anatase phase with the increase of the synthesis temperature (Fig. 1(c)). The phase compositions were estimated by the proportion of area under the XRD peaks at 14.79° and 38° for (B)-phase and anatase, respectively. The (B)-phase content was 80% and 18% for samples synthesized at 170 °C and 180 °C, respectively.

Crystal phase formation was further confirmed with the Raman shift as shown in Fig. 2 . At 150 °C and 160 °C, major vibrational peaks belonged to (B)-TiO2, typically at 250 cm−1 [7,19]. At 170 °C, (A) peaks (the intense peak at 148 cm−1) were co-existent with (B)-phase [7,18]. By increasing the temperature to 180 °C and 190 °C, most of the (B)-phase peaks disappeared, and the remaining peaks were for the (A)-phase. Raman data were consistent with the XRD data, in that pure (B)-phase was obtained at reaction temperatures of 150 °C and 160 °C. Mixed (A/B) phase was observed at 170 °C and 180 °C. At higher temperatures from 190 °C, (B)-phase was completely transformed to (A)-phase.

The effects of reaction duration on phase formation were investigated next. At reaction temperatures of 150, 160 and 170 °C, the reaction time was extended to 36 h. Pure (B)-phase was still obtained at 150 °C and 160 °C, and mixed (A/B)-phase was observed at 170 °C (see Supplementary Information, Fig. S1). Reaction times of 5, 12, 24 and 36 h were examined with samples synthesized at 180 °C. Pure (B)-phase was formed at 5 and 12 h; however, XRD peaks for (A) were observed at 24 h (Fig. S2). XRD peaks for (A) were only observed when the reaction time was extended to 36 h, which indicated that (B)-TiO2 was preferentially formed from thermal treatment of TiCl4 in EG solution at low temperature (<170 °C). (B)-phase was not stable at high temperature, and was transformed to (A) at higher temperatures or for longer reaction times. (B)-phase was found to start transforming to (A) from 170 °C.

Beside TiO2 formation, the thermally reacted solution (TiCl4 + EG) also showed different colors with different reaction temperatures. Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show photographs of crude solutions under room-light and UV-light after solvothermal treatment for 24 h. Under room-light, white color was observed with 150 °C giving a mixture of formed TiO2 and transparent EG solution. The color changed to light-brown and then dark brown with an increase of the temperature to 160, 170, 180, 190 and 200 °C, respectively. This solution emitted green color under UV light radiation, which implies that CDs possibly formed from decomposition of EG under thermal treatment. To confirm the origin of this emission, solvothermal reaction of EG solution was performed at 170 °C for 24 h without TiCl4. A similar result to that with the TiCl4 precursor was observed. Thermally treated EG solution was diluted in water and it emitted a bright green color under UV-light radiation (Fig. S3). Therefore, the green color of the crude solutions is considered to be due to CDs from thermally treated EG.

For further confirmation of CD formation, PL measurements were conducted. Colloidal particulates synthesized at 170 °C, the mixed (A/B)-phase, were removed from the crude solution by centrifugation and re-dispersed into water, termed as (A/B)-TiO2/CDs. Remaining reacted EG solution was diluted in water, and was considered to be pure CDs (CD solution). As a control, the prepared (A) powder (home-made) was dispersed into water (TiO2 solution). PL emission of Ti170 was observed in a broad range of the visible region (430 to 550 nm), excited with 300 nm, 350 nm, 400 nm and 450 nm light (Fig. 3(c) and 3(d)). The most intense emission was from excitation at 400 nm. There was no such emission with the pure TiO2 solution, while the pure CD solution had the same emission range as Ti170 (Fig. 3(e)). However, the intensity of the CD-solution was much higher than that of the Ti170 solution. A similar trend of emission was observed with NIR excitation at 800 nm (Fig. 3(f)). This indicates the up/down-converting emission characteristics of CDs, which can absorb in the UV to NIR range [16,17]. Therefore, it was concluded that CDs were formed by the decomposition of EG in the solvothermal reaction. Brown color of solution was inferred to be CD formation.

Many works have used a similar process to synthesize (B)-TiO2; however, none of them claimed that CDs were formed. We investigated the formation of CDs further by varying the reaction duration. Almost no green emission was observed for samples synthesized at 150 °C, although the reaction was extended to 36 h (Fig. S4(a)). The reaction temperature was fixed at 180 °C, and reaction times of 5 to 12, 24, and 36 h were investigated. No CDs were formed at short reaction time (<12 h), which indicated that CD formation from EG decomposition by the solvothermal method occurred at high temperature (>170 °C) for longer reaction times (>12 h). A similar process was used by several researchers such as Kong et al. [20] and Xiang et al. [1] (at 150 °C for 4 h), Liu et al. (at 150 °C for 24 h) [2], and Di et al. (at 180 °C for 1 h) [5]. However, CD formation was not reported, because their synthesis reactions were performed at lower temperatures and/or for short reaction times.

Further characterization of the nanostructures of the materials synthesized with 24 h reaction at different temperatures was conducted using electron microscopy. Fig. 4 shows SEM images with irregular-shape particles composed of thin nanosheets. Similar structures were observed for the samples synthesized at 150, 160 and 170 °C. However, when the reaction temperature was increased to 180 °C, small particulates (not sheet-like) began to form, and at 190 °C, only small particulate structures were evident; the nanosheet-structured particles had disappeared. (B)-TiO2 synthesized by the solvothermal method was formed as nanosheet particles with the assistance of NH3 solution [1,2]. For details of the nanostructures, TEM and HR-TEM analyses were conducted. The results of three samples (Ti170, Ti180 and Ti190) are shown in Fig. 5 . Very thin sheet-structures were observed with the Ti170 sample (Fig. 5(a)) and almost no particulate-structures were observed. As the temperature was increased to 180 °C, more particulate-structures were formed, and the nanosheet-structures were completely transformed to particulates at a temperature 190 °C (Fig. 5(b) and (c)). It was difficult to observe the CDs with low magnification TEM. Therefore, HR-TEM observations were conducted to identify the crystal phases of the Ti170 sample. Lattice distances of two different places in Fig. 5(d) were 0.353 nm and 0.329 nm, corresponding to plan (101) and (002) of anatase and carbon dots, respectively [16,17]. Lattices distances in Fig. 5(g) were measured to 0.359 nm and 0.328 nm, which were indexed to plane (110) and (002) of (B)-TiO2 and carbon dots [3]. Further, selected area diffractive patterns obtained by fast-Fourier-transform (FFT) were used to re-check the phases of the samples. In Fig. 5(d)–(i), that the selected area diffraction patterns from the Ti170 sample were indexed to (A), (B)-phase and CDs. TEM analysis confirmed that the sample synthesized at 170 °C was composed of (A), (B)-phase and CDs.

The surface elemental states of the Ti170 sample obtained from full-range XPS spectra were composed of Ti, O, C (Fig. 6 (a)). There were no impurities of other elements detected. High-resolution XPS spectra of all elements were deconvoluted and are plotted in Fig. 6(b)–(d). Strong peaks at 458.5 and 464.2 eV originated from the binding energies of Ti 2p (2p3/2 and 2p1/2) [18] (Fig. 5(b)). A broad peak at 528–535 eV was deconvoluted into three sub-peaks (Fig. 6(c)). The most intense peak at 529.8 eV belongs to the lattice oxygen of TiO2 (OTi-O). Smaller peaks at 531.4 and 533 eV were originated from surface hydroxyl oxygen (OOH) and chemisorbed oxygen (OOab), respectively [18]. In Fig. 6(d), a broad C 1 s peak was observed at 287–290 eV, which was composed of C–C bonds with sp2 orbitals (ca. 284.8 eV), C-O bonds (ca. 286 eV) and C-OH bonds (ca. 288.5 eV). It was generally observed that sp2 peak at ~284.8 eV was typical characteristics of graphitic structure of CDs [16,21,22].

Fig. 7 (a) shows UV–vis absorption spectra for the samples synthesized at different temperatures. All samples synthesized at 150 to 170 °C showed an absorption band at around 360 nm, which implies the bandgap absorption of TiO2. A small red-shift was observed (compared with that of 150 and 160 °C) as the temperature was increased from 160 to 170 °C, and the visible absorption (>400 nm) was much more pronounced for synthesis temperatures of 180 and 190 °C. This absorption behavior corresponds to CD formation with an increase of the temperature because CDs absorb a broad range of wavelengths from UV to NIR. Pure (A)-phase showed no visible light absorption (>400 nm). Bandgap energies were predicted based on UV–vis absorption spectra by the Tauc function [23], and are plotted in Fig. 7(b). Bandgap energies of samples synthesized at 150 and 160 °C were identical (ca. 3.35 eV), while they were slowly reduced to 3.27, 3.22 and 3.12 eV with the increase of temperatures to 170, 180 and 190 °C, respectively. This reduction may be due to the presence of (A)-phase with a smaller bandgap. The bandgap of controlled (A) was similar to the sample synthesized at 190 °C, which was pure (A)-phase.

Photocatalytic activities of synthesized catalysts were evaluated by H2 evolution reaction from EG-water solution (20 vol% of EG) under a Hg-Xe lamp (UV–visible light). H2 production over the Ti150 and Ti160 catalysts was not much different (Fig. 8 (a)). However, H2 generation was increased significantly with the Ti170 sample. H2 generation was not further increased for the catalysts synthesized at 180 and 190 °C. The H2 production rates were 0.004, 0.003, 0.011, 0.005 and 0.09 mmol h−1 for catalysts Ti150, Ti160, Ti170, Ti180 and Ti190, respectively (Fig. 8(b)). Comparison of these H2 production rates indicates that the sample composed of (B)-phase, (A) and CDs [(A/B)-TiO2/CDs], i.e., that synthesized at 170 °C, had the highest photocatalytic activity. This production rate of (A/B)-TiO2/CDs was 3.6 times higher than that of pure (A). The lower photocatalytic H2 generation over Ti180 may be due to a higher concentration of CDs, which are considered as light-absorbing competitors with the active catalyst (TiO2), and accumulation of CDs on the surface of the catalyst reduces the active sites [16]. The Ti190 sample showed a much higher photocatalytic activity than that of Ti180, although Ti180 is a mixed phase. This is due to the difference in the crystallinity and particle structure (see Fig. 4). The photocurrent densities in Fig. 8(c) show a similar trend to the photocatalytic activities. The current density with (A/B)-TiO2/CDs was much higher than that of pure (B) at the initial time. However, after running for 300 s, the current density with (A/B)-TiO2/CDs was almost the same as that with (A)-TiO2/CD. The fast reduction of photocurrent densities at the initial time of measurement was observed with Ti170 and Ti150. This should be due to the stability of Ti150 and Ti170 films prepared by simple solution method. Ti150 and Ti170 samples composted of nanosheet structures assembled into bigger particles with un-uniform size-distribution. Therefore, un-uniform distribution of particles on FTO-glass was expected. Due to bigger sizes and un-uniform distribution of particles on substrate, catalyst powder was easily de-attached from substrate when measurement was started. In difference from Ti150 and Ti170, Ti190 had smaller particles and more uniform sizes. So, the distribution of particles on the substrate would be more well dispersed. With its smaller particle sizes and uniform distribution, Ti190 film should be more stable. Therefore, we observed the higher photocurrent density of Ti170 at the initial time. The photocatalytic H2 generation is summarized in the order (B/A)-TiO2/CDs (170 °C) > (A)-TiO2/CDs (190 °C) > (B)-TiO2 (150 & 160 °C) > (A).

The enhanced photocatalytic H2 generation in this composite material is explained by the proposed mechanism in Fig. 8(d). The conduction band maximum (CBM) of (A) is higher than that of (B)-TiO2 (more negative) [5,7,8]. Based on the bandgap energies determined from UV–vis absorption spectra (Eg(B) = 3.35 eV, and Eg(A) = 3.18), the valence band maximum (VBM) of (B)-phase must be more positive than that of (A). Therefore, excited electrons are thermodynamically transferred from (A) to (B)-phase, while holes are transferred from (B)-phase to (A), which results in effective charge separation [7,8]. In addition, a heterostructure with CDs would further enhance electron transfer from (A/B)-TiO2 to CDs, which is an additional charge separation. CDs could also harvest the visible light and transfer electrons to the CBM of TiO2 for further utilization in photocatalysis [15–17]. Therefore, (A/B)-TiO2/CDs exhibited higher photocatalytic activities under UV–visible light than pure (B)-TiO2, (A)-TiO2/CDs and pure (A) due to broad light absorption and effective charge separation.

To increase the rate of H2 production, non-noble metals such as Ni, Cu and Co were loaded onto the catalysts by an in-situ photoreductive method [24]. Optimization experiments were performed to obtain the optimum loading concentration of metal nanoparticles. The optimum loading concentrations were determined to be 0.1 at% for Cu and Co, and 1 at% for Ni (data not shown). Loading with these metals improved H2 production significantly (Fig. 9 ). Within 7 h of photoreaction, total H2 generation was increased from 0.078 mmol (Ti170) to 0.229, 0.281 and 0.282 mmol for catalyst loading with Ni (Ti170/Ni), Cu (Ti170/Cu) and Co (Ti170/Co), respectively (Fig. 9(a)). It is well understood that loading metal nanoparticles improves the H2-production reaction over semiconductor photocatalysts based on two main factors: 1) reduction of the activation energy (overpotential) for the H2-evolution reaction on the surface of a semiconductor and 2) improvement of the electron-hole separation [25]. The average H2-production rate was increased from 0.011 mmol h−1 to 0.033, 0.040 and 0.040 mmol h−1 for Ti170 samples loaded with Ni, Co and Cu, respectively (Fig. 9(b)). Finally, the stability and recyclability of the best catalyst sample, Ti170/Cu, were tested. Fig. 9(c) shows 4 catalytic cycles performed with a total reaction time of 16 h. H2 production was reduced by 5% after 16 h of reaction. The catalyst was highly stable, and it is thus considered to be suitable for H2 production under UV–visible light.

To demonstrate the performance of the catalysts synthesized by this method, H2 evolution from the present and previous work with a similar catalyst and co-catalysts was compared. Table S1 shows that the present samples under UV–visible light were comparable or more active than (A) or pure (B) phase and (A/B)-TiO2 heterostructures reported previously [7,16,20,26,27]. However, the present samples showed less activity than P25 and defective TiO2 reported in references [12,17]. In terms of facile synthesis, the process suggested here is considered more promising than multiple-step methods reported in comparable works.

A one-pot solvothermal method with TiCl4 and EG solution was successfully used to produce (B)-TiO2, (A/B)-TiO2/CDs and (A)-TiO2/CDs by control of the reaction temperature and duration. (B)-TiO2 was preferentially formed at lower reaction temperature (150 and 160 °C), and (B)-TiO2 was transformed to (A) at reaction temperatures from 170 °C. Besides phase transformation, CD formation from the decomposition of EG by thermal treatment at high temperatures (>160 °C) and for longer reaction times (>12 h) was also confirmed. Therefore, the (A/B)-TiO2/CD photocatalyst with high photocatalytic was successfully synthesized at 170 °C for 24 h. The H2 evolution rate obtained from EG aqueous solution under UV–visible light showed that (A/B)-TiO2/CDs was the best catalyst. The higher photocatalytic activity of this sample toward H2 production was due to effective charge-separation and broad light absorption. H2 production was significantly improved with non-noble (Cu, Co and Ni) metal loading. The average production rates were 0.033, 0.040 and 0.040 mmol h−1 for (A/B)-TiO2/CDs loaded with Ni, Co and Cu, respectively. This developed method has potential for the synthesis of heterostructural materials because it is simple (one-pot method) and requires fewer chemicals.

Sovann Khan: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yuta Kubota: Formal analysis, Investigation. Wenwei Lei: Investigation, Data curation. Norihito Suzuki: Investigation, Methodology. Kazuya Nakata: Methodology. Chiaki Terashima: Investigation, Methodology. Nobuhiro Matsushita: Formal analysis, Investigation. Akira Fujishima: Conceptualization, Methodology. Ken-ichi Katsumata: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

The authors thank Technical Department, Ookayama Materials Analysis Division, Tokyo Institute of Technology, for performing the FE-TEM observation. S. Khan would like to thank JSPS for supporting his postdoctoral research (standard-P18337).
